scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The play Plutus satirizes the socio-political conditions of 4th-century BCE Athens, specifically the functioning of the Ekklesia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristophanes' Plutus was produced in 388 BCE, reflecting contemporary Athenian democratic practices.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'trierarchy' mentioned was a specific liturgy of the Athenian state where wealthy citizens were required to fund warships.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play was performed in 388 BCE; the trierarchy remained a central, albeit burdensome, institution of that time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'three obols' refers specifically to the ekklesiastikon, the attendance fee for the Athenian Assembly in the 4th century BCE.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The increase of the assembly fee to three obols occurred in the 390s BCE, shortly before the play's production in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mention of 'buying the orators' silence' refers to the professionalized legal culture of 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated in Aristophanes' play performed in 388 BCE, describing contemporary corruption and litigation norms.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The practice of using family as supplicants in court trials (the tribunal) is a well-documented tactic in Athenian legal oratory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects Athenian courtroom theatrics contemporary with the play's production in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Shackles and the use of physical force were standard methods of slave control and punishment in the Athenian household.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The date reflects the play's production in 388 BCE, portraying the contemporary social reality for Athenian slaves.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The reliance on slave labor for manual tasks and specialized trades was a structural economic feature of 4th-century Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the economic organization of Athens at the time of the play's writing in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The festival of Theseus (Theseia) was an Athenian civic-religious event that included distributions to the poor.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play refers to this distribution as a known contemporary or recently established urban norm in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the behavior of orators (politicians) within the Athenian democratic republic.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Poverty's speech satirizes the political figures of the day in 388 BCE Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Chattel slavery, including the purchase of slaves in a market setting, was the standard in Classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The dialogue reflects the commodity nature of labor in Athens during Aristophanes' lifetime, specifically 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule 'ho boulomenos' (whoever wishes) was a foundational principle of the Athenian legal system for public actions.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This legal principle was a core feature of the restored Athenian democracy in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The torture of slaves (basanos) was a legal requirement for their testimony to be valid or admissible in Athenian courts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This refers to a contemporary and brutal legal norm of the late 5th and early 4th centuries BCE in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Informer' (Sykophantes) claims the right of active citizenship to oversee state morals, a hallmark of radical Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The character reflects the lived experience of litigation culture in Athens in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athenian jurors (dikastai) were paid a daily wage, and the use of 'names' refers to the complex assignment system to various courts.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the late democratic jury system where payment was a primary motivation for poor elderly citizens in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Greek religious custom, and Athenian practice specifically, entitled priests to certain portions (gera) of sacrificed animals.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The interaction describes traditional religious norms as observed in Athens during the play's production in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: In the Athenian household, slaves were subject to the physical discipline of their masters and bore liability for their own errors.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play depicts contemporary domestic power dynamics in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The lottery system for assigning jurors (dikastai) to specific courts (indicated by letters) is a characteristic of 4th-century Athenian democracy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text describes the intricate juror assignment system described by Aristophanes in 388 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Athens granted certain exemptions to 'emporoi' (maritime traders) to ensure the steady supply of grain and goods to the city.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The play and its scholia describe exemptions relevant to the economic stability of Athens in the 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between 'dike' (private) and 'graphe' (public) suits is a fundamental mechanism of Athenian law.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule details the specific penalties and procedural rules of the 4th-century BCE Athenian court system.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The 'Hecate's Suppers' (Hekateia deipna) were offerings made by the wealthy at crossroads, which the poor were permitted to consume.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes a long-standing Athenian socio-religious custom existing at the time of the play's production in 388 BCE.
